label: Battery
scenario_patterns: A security guard physically pushes a shopper out of a store without justification
A medical practitioner performs a procedure without obtaining the patient's informed consent
A person spits on another during a heated argument in a hawker centre
An employer grabs an employee's arm aggressively during a workplace dispute
legal_tests: The defendant intentionally applied force to the plaintiff's person
The contact was direct or indirect (including through an instrument or object set in motion by the defendant)
The plaintiff did not consent to the contact
The force need not cause injury — any intentional, unwanted physical contact suffices
The defendant must have intended the act of contact, though not necessarily the resulting harm
common_fact_patterns: Altercations in public places such as hawker centres, MRT stations, or HDB void decks
Medical procedures performed without valid informed consent
Excessive use of force by security personnel or bouncers
Workplace physical altercations between colleagues or employer and employee
Unwanted physical contact in the context of domestic disputes
sg_case_references: Aries Telecoms (M) Bhd v ViewQwest Pte Ltd [2020] SGCA 12 — intentional torts including battery discussed in SG context
Wee Sia Tian v Long Thik Boon [1996] SGHC 84 — battery arising from a physical assault
Koh Sak Peow v Yeo Khee Siang [1996] SGHC 141 — battery and assessment of damages for physical assault
Re Kong Chian & Ors [1965] MLJ 81 — early authority on battery elements in the local context
party_roles: employer vicariously liable for employee's battery
defences: Consent — the plaintiff consented to the contact (express or implied, e.g., in sports)
Self-defence — reasonable force used to defend oneself or another
Defence of property — reasonable force to protect one's property
Lawful authority — force applied under statutory or lawful power (e.g., lawful arrest)
Necessity — force applied to prevent greater harm
